message.text: found an issue with the table display. For a hex file with gap, as in testdata_with_gap.hex, the rows for those addresses in the gap should be shown too

parts.text: found an issue with the table display. For a hex file with gap, as in testdata_with_gap.hex, the rows for those addresses in the gap should be shown too

response.value: Looking at the code, the issue is in

. It only creates rows for addresses that actually exist in the file:

```python
self.row_bases = sorted({a & ~0x0F for a in self.mem.keys()})
```

This skips rows in the gap. You need to fill in all rows between the min and max row base, regardless of whether data exists there.

````python

# ...existing code...
        self.mem = self.ih.todict()
        
        if self.mem:
            min_base = min(self.mem.keys()) & ~0x0F
            max_base = max(self.mem.keys()) & ~0x0F
            self.row_bases = list(range(min_base, max_base + 0x10, 0x10))
        else:
            self.row_bases = []
        # ...existing code...
````

This replaces the single line:
```python
self.row_bases = sorted({a & ~0x0F for a in self.mem.keys()})
```

Now all 16-byte rows between the first and last address will be shown. Cells in the gap will display `FF` (the default in
) and the ASCII column will show dots for those bytes, which matches typical hex viewer behavior for unpopulated regions.

metadata.helpTextPrefix: You can ask me general programming questions, or chat with the following participants which have specialized expertise and can perform actions:

helpTextPostfix.value:
To have a great conversation, ask me questions as if I was a real programmer:

* **Show me the code** you want to talk about by having the files open and selecting the most important lines.
* **Make refinements** by asking me follow-up questions, adding clarifications, providing errors, etc.
* **Review my suggested code** and tell me about issues or improvements, so I can iterate on it.

You can also ask me questions about your editor selection by [starting an inline chat session](command:inlineChat.start).

Learn more about [GitHub Copilot](https://docs.github.com/copilot/using-github-copilot/getting-started-with-github-copilot?tool=vscode&utm_source=editor&utm_medium=chat-panel&utm_campaign=2024q3-em-MSFT-getstarted) in [Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/overview). Or explore the [Copilot walkthrough](command:github.copilot.open.walkthrough).

toolCallRounds.response:
Looking at the code, the issue is in `populate_table`. It only creates rows for addresses that actually exist in the file:

```python
self.row_bases = sorted({a & ~0x0F for a in self.mem.keys()})
```

This skips rows in the gap. You need to fill in all rows between the min and max row base, regardless of whether data exists there.

````python
# filepath: e:\xboot\mhwisp\wx_test\hex_viewer.py
        # ...existing code...
        self.mem = self.ih.todict()
        
        if self.mem:
            min_base = min(self.mem.keys()) & ~0x0F
            max_base = max(self.mem.keys()) & ~0x0F
            self.row_bases = list(range(min_base, max_base + 0x10, 0x10))
        else:
            self.row_bases = []
        # ...existing code...
````

This replaces the single line:
```python
self.row_bases = sorted({a & ~0x0F for a in self.mem.keys()})
```

Now all 16-byte rows between the first and last address will be shown. Cells in the gap will display `FF` (the default in `mem.get(addr, 0xFF)`) and the ASCII column will show dots for those bytes, which matches typical hex viewer behavior for unpopulated regions.
